# Questions in Livestream 61

Live Stream #61: Understanding Hell, There Will Be A Test.

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=573" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:33</a> | Why would a blemish in Leviticus 21:16-21 violate sacred space? | He answers that a holy God requires unblemished sacrifices and physically whole priests for temple service, so priestly defects disqualify ritual participation. | He reads the Leviticus passage as specifically about Aaronic priests, not ordinary Israelites. His explanation is that the standard of wholeness applied both to offerings and to those who performed the offerings: a holy God required holy service, so the priest serving in sacred space had to be physically intact just as the sacrificial animal had to be unblemished. He compares this to broader ancient ideas that divine service demanded physically ideal priests or kings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=872" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:32</a> | If people in hell still have free will, can they change their mind and go to heaven? | He says no in the ordinary sense: their final disposition is fixed, though they may still choose actions that increase or lessen their suffering. | He distinguishes free will from the ability to reverse one's final destiny. In his answer, the condemned have already fixed their disposition, but they may still choose whether to keep sinning in ways that worsen their misery or to do less. He illustrates this with kings in Sheol who are lethargic and inactive: they are still choosing, but within the limits of a state they have already entered. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=1073" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:53</a> | In the Old Testament demons seem to participate in the divine council. Does that change, and if so why? | His view is that this ends at Christ's resurrection, when the demons lose standing in the divine council and move to opposing God's work on earth instead. | He agrees that demons have access to the divine council in the Old Testament and notes Job as a late example. He differs from views that place their expulsion at events such as Babylon's fall, arguing instead that the resurrection is the turning point: once Christ rises, they are defeated, have no standing in the council, and can only try to delay the fullness of the Gentiles by causing havoc on earth. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=1283" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:23</a> | If Judges 1:1 is after Joshua's death, why is Joshua still alive in Judges 2:6-8? | He says Judges 2:6-8 inserts an earlier event as literary context, reminding readers what happened before the judges cycle unfolded. | He explains that Judges begins after Joshua's death but then inserts earlier material to orient the reader. Judges 2:6-8 is not being presented as chronologically later than Judges 1:1; it reminds readers that Israel served the Lord during Joshua's generation, then reverted after Joshua and the elders died. He frames the passage as literary setup for the book's cycle and for the ordered contrast between good and bad judges. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=1645" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:25</a> | Do John 5:25 and 1 Peter 3:19 imply that Jesus preached to and saved souls in Sheol, including those on the bad side? | He says Christ preached grace to the saved, and may also have preached judgment to the condemned, but not salvation to the lost side of Sheol. | He appeals to the Lazarus and rich man account to describe Sheol as having a good side and a bad side separated by a gulf. He thinks Jesus may have preached grace to the saved souls, and it is possible that the condemned also heard a message, but that message would be law or judgment rather than rescue. He stresses that preaching can function as both grace to the humble and law to the proud. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=1874" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:14</a> | When moving very heavy cargo, did ancient Egyptians use four-wheeled wagons or only two-wheeled carts? | He says wheeled carts appear late and rarely in Egypt; for the heaviest loads the Egyptians usually relied on sledges pulled by oxen. | He says wheeled carts are poorly attested in Egypt until very late, with iconographic evidence appearing in the late period, and he doubts they were common in the New Kingdom. For massive loads such as obelisks, he says Egyptians used boats and sledges because sledges could handle far more weight than carts. He adds that wood was expensive and more profitably used for chariots, so carts would have been rare luxury equipment rather than ordinary heavy-hauling tools. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=2117" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">35:17</a> | Can you explain Daniel 9's seventy weeks, when they start, and how they end? | He ties the starting point to Cyrus's decree connected with rebuilding Jerusalem, placing the clock's beginning in 539 BC. | He reads Daniel 9 as starting with the decree to restore and rebuild Jerusalem, then connects that decree to Ezra's reference to Cyrus authorizing the rebuilding of the house of God. Because Ezra specifies Cyrus's first year as king of Babylon, he dates the start to Cyrus's conquest of Babylon in 539 BC. He leaves the rest of the calculation to the questioner, focusing this answer on the starting point. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=2351" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:11</a> | Is there archaeological evidence for events in the book of Judges? | He says there is some, especially the Jerubbaal inscription and broader settlement evidence for Israelite occupation in the judges period. | He points first to the Jerubbaal inscription from roughly the judges period, emphasizing that the name is significant because it is pejorative and matches a name known from Judges. He also cites broader settlement evidence, with many new Israelite settlements appearing during the conquest and judges period. He cautions that the period is relatively short, so detailed confirmation is difficult, but says the available evidence supports at least a good portion of the setting and events. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=2523" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">42:03</a> | Can biblical prophecy be intentionally ambiguous, with both near and far fulfillment at once? | Yes. He says this kind of double reference is common in ancient prophetic writing and gives Daniel 8 as an example. | He says yes and identifies the literary device as amphiboly, writing with two intentions at the same time. He treats Daniel 8's little horn as a possible near reference to Antiochus and a later reference to Antichrist, then argues that this kind of deliberate ambiguity appears widely in ancient Near Eastern prophetic literature. He gives Egyptian prophetic writing as a parallel, where a text can be intentionally readable in more than one way. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=2726" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:26</a> | Joel 3:6 says Jews were sold to the Greeks by Tyre, Sidon, and Philistia. When would that have happened, and did Jews even know the Greeks then? | He argues the text fits later Greek awareness and contact, especially once Greeks became more visible to the eastern Mediterranean world. | He says Joel's date is disputed, which makes the question difficult. Greeks were trading around the Mediterranean by the seventh century BC, had a colony at Naucratis, and were known to Egypt even earlier, so awareness was possible by the late pre-exilic period and much easier by the sixth century. He is less confident about the ninth century and says the book's internal evidence does not settle the date, so the answer remains partly inferential. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=3079" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">51:19</a> | What is your opinion of Andrew Henry and Religion for Breakfast? | He says he does not have an opinion because he has not watched the channel, though he has received requests to review it. | He says he knows Andrew Henry is a YouTuber and has received requests to do popcorn reviews of Religion for Breakfast, but he has never actually watched the channel. Because of that, he refuses to give a substantive opinion beyond saying he does not know enough to evaluate it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=3169" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:49</a> | Is an allegorical understanding of Adam and Eve acceptable? | He says some figurative and temple language is present, but he does not think a fully allegorical reading is the best understanding. | He says Genesis uses figurative and temple language, with Eden functioning like an earthly temple and Adam and Eve presented in priestly terms, but he still thinks the account intends a real Adam, Eve, sin, fall, and expulsion. He allows that some details may be shaped to emphasize the temple setting, yet he is wary of allegorical readings because they can become slippery. A given allegory might be acceptable within orthodoxy, but he does not think allegory is the best reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=3568" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:28</a> | Did Israel borrow the pork taboo from Egyptian culture and then put it into Torah as Yahweh's command? | He says Egyptian and Israelite practice overlap here, but grounds Israel's food laws in Torah rather than reducing them to a simple borrowing claim. | He says God's commands engage the culture Israel already knew rather than appearing as meaningless abstractions. In that sense, if Israelites were already not eating pig, the Torah could confirm that practice as good and tell them to continue it when surrounded by neighbors who did eat pork. He frames the law as Yahweh evaluating and redirecting Israel's existing cultural practices, not merely copying Egypt. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=3868" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:04:28</a> | Did Akhenaten's monotheistic cult revive older Egyptian ideas about the rulership of Re/ Aten, or is that too speculative? | He says that reconstruction is too speculative and explicitly disagrees with Hoffmeier on that point. | He respectfully disagrees with Hoffmeier and says Akhenaten was not a monotheist but a monologist pantheist, because the Aten hymns make everything a manifestation of Aten. He grants that Aten worship is loosely connected to the Re cult because the Aten began as the solar disc of Re, but he says the Amarna cult cannot be securely reconstructed as an extension of Old Kingdom Re-Atum worship. The problem is that the Old Kingdom evidence, especially for Heliopolis, is too thin and the later architecture looks very different. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=4229" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:29</a> | How much influence did Egyptians have on the Greeks, or Greeks on Egyptians? | He says the answer depends on period, but later Greek contact with Egypt clearly mattered, especially through trade, tourism, and intellectual exchange. | He says the answer changes by period. Egyptians knew of Greeks early, Greeks founded Naucratis in the seventh century, and Greek visitors took ideas from Egyptian religious thought into philosophy. After Alexander and the Ptolemies, influence became more two-way: Greek law, contracts, coinage, and language affected Egypt, while Egyptian gods, medicine, and cults such as Isis and Serapis spread through the Greek world. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=4636" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:16</a> | How would First Temple Israelites have understood prophecy if it was not deterministic? | He says they treated prophecy as genuinely conditional, with outcomes responsive to human action rather than mechanically fixed. | He argues that many prophecies are conditional rather than deterministic, using David's decision not to go to Jerusalem after being warned that Saul would capture him as an example. The warning was true if David took that path, but David's response changed the outcome. He also warns that prophecy only promises what it actually says, and gives Ahaz as another case where disobedience changes which sign is received. Some prophecies are unconditional, but many depend on obedience and circumstance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=5548" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:28</a> | Why did the Judahite monarchy keep an affinity with Egypt despite the Exodus and later warnings in Isaiah and Kings? | He says Egypt remained an attractive geopolitical counterweight to stronger empires, even though relying on it was repeatedly condemned. | He traces Judah's attraction to Egypt back to Solomon's treaty and marriage alliance, then says Egypt remained Judah's nearest major potential ally when Assyria and Babylon threatened the region. From a worldly perspective, Judah feared it could not survive alone, but the prophets warned that Egypt was unreliable and spiritually dangerous. He frames the repeated turn to Egypt as fear-driven failure to trust God, ending in renewed Egyptianization and religious compromise. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=6099" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:39</a> | If Jacob was renamed Israel in Egypt, why say there were no Israelites in Egypt? | He answers that Jacob is called Israel, but the people were not yet identified as Israelites in the later covenantal sense and were labeled differently in Egypt. | He distinguishes the sons of Israel from Israelites as a unified covenant nation. In Egypt, he says they would have been called Asiatics or Amu, and Avaris itself was a multicultural city with Semites, Nubians, Libyans, Egyptians, and others. The people leave as a mixed multitude and only become Israel as a holy nation after the exodus and Sinai, when that mixed multitude is grafted into the twelve tribes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=6775" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:52:55</a> | What are your thoughts on the discovery of General Wahibre-mery-Neith? | He says he only has the news brief and is waiting for more official detail, but notes the burial and many shabtis suggest a wealthy late 26th- or early 27th-dynasty figure. | He says he has only seen a news brief and is waiting for more official details, so his answer is deliberately limited. From what he has seen, the figure dates to the late 26th or early 27th Dynasty, around the early fifth century BC, and may have commanded foreign mercenaries. He notes that the large number of shabtis implies substantial wealth, but he does not yet know enough to say more confidently. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=6944" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:44</a> | What would a New Kingdom Egyptian know about Egypt's origins, unification wars, predynastic kings, and Khufu's pyramid? | He says they knew Khufu built the Great Pyramid and remembered unification, but predynastic royal memory had largely dropped out or become mythologized. | He says New Kingdom Egyptians knew Khufu built the Great Pyramid, partly because later figures such as Khaemwaset restored and documented Giza monuments. They also remembered the unification of the two lands through symbols, royal lists, and the sema-tawy motif. However, predynastic kings had largely fallen out of accurate memory, with some early history transformed into myths such as the conflict of Horus and Seth. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=7467" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:04:27</a> | What do you think of Simo Parpola, and have you read "The Assyrian Tree of Life: Tracing the Origins of Jewish Monotheism"? | He has not read the paper and cannot evaluate Parpola's argument directly, but says the tree of life motif is widespread and he grounds Jewish monotheism in Torah. | He says he has not read Parpola's paper and does not know enough about Parpola's views to evaluate the argument. He can comment only on the tree of life as a motif, which he says is ubiquitous across the ancient Near East and appears all over Egyptian material. His own view is that Jewish monotheism goes back to the Ten Commandments and Torah, so he is cautious about deriving it from an Assyrian tree-of-life argument he has not read. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=7747" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:09:07</a> | Who is your favorite guitarist? | He says he follows songs and guitar parts more than individual players, names music by Led Zeppelin, the Eagles, Nirvana, "Scarborough Fair," and says Bach adapts especially well to guitar. | He says he does not follow individual guitarists closely and remembers sounds more than names. He enjoys guitar parts in Led Zeppelin, the Eagles, Nirvana, "Scarborough Fair," and especially "Hotel California." He then says Bach may be his favorite composer to play on guitar because Bach's music adapts surprisingly well to the instrument, even though Bach himself did not play guitar. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=8130" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:30</a> | Will you convert to Catholicism soon? | He says he does not disclose denominational leanings because he wants to avoid channel strife and preserve freedom to critique all denominations fairly. | He answers jokingly that even if he did, he would not say so, then explains his policy. He avoids naming his denominational leanings because doing so would invite partisan reactions and make critique of churches look biased. He wants the channel's dialogue to stay civil and says he is a conservative Christian committed to Christ, the gospel, and the Bible first, with denominational distinctives kept secondary. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=8546" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:22:26</a> | Is the theory that the Sphinx is far older than usually dated credible, and is Graham Hancock a charlatan? | He rejects the old-Sphinx theory as crank material and calls Hancock an unreliable popularizer of fringe ideas. | He rejects the theory and dates the Sphinx to the time of Khafre in Dynasty 4. He says claims based on erosion fail because nearby monuments such as Menkaure's temple show how severe erosion can be on Giza limestone, with alternating hard and soft layers affected by wind-driven sand. He also dismisses Hancock as a popularizer of fringe ancient-technology ideas rather than a reliable source. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=8738" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:25:38</a> | Why do we not have pyramid construction blueprints, and how were the stones cut and moved? | He says the Egyptians did not work to fantasy-level precision, used abrasion techniques to flatten stone, and that the documentary record simply has not preserved blueprints. | He says the stones were not cut with impossible precision; rough stone could be abraded flat, then finished after placement, as seen in unfinished columns and pyramid masonry. For transport, smaller stones moved by sledge and very large loads moved by boat, with canals and ports bringing boats near construction areas. Egypt did use some blueprints for other works, but pyramid blueprints have not survived, and the Red Sea papyri describe construction logistics rather than preserve pyramid plans. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=9088" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:31:28</a> | Doesn't the Sphinx show rainwater erosion? | He says no: the visible pattern is overwhelmingly wind erosion, not rainfall erosion. | He says the Sphinx and Giza plateau do not receive enough rain to explain the erosion pattern. Wind erosion happens continuously, with sand acting like a sandblaster, and can be more damaging over time than rare water events. He acknowledges that tiny amounts of water erosion are possible, but says the visible damage, especially on the Sphinx's hindquarters and other sites, is overwhelmingly from wind and sand. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=9363" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:36:03</a> | What is your favorite type of cheese? | He names a French cheese and explains that he developed a taste for it while living in Liverpool and buying marked-down cheeses. | He names a French soft cheese that he describes as a thick, very soft brie-like round. He says he developed a taste for cheeses while doing his PhD in Liverpool, where marked-down brie and similar cheeses were affordable, and tells a story about later asking a Paris cheesemonger how to pronounce the cheese's name. He says he eventually learned the pronunciation from a cheese specialist and still considers it delicious. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=9723" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:42:03</a> | If some pre-Exodus Hebrew names look Yahwistic, does that show Yahweh worship before the Exodus? | He says not necessarily, because names can be edited or retheophorized later and the evidence does not prove pre-Exodus Yahweh worship by itself. | He says some names that now look Yahwistic may have been changed after the exodus to remove pagan theophoric elements or to make them consistent with Yahweh worship. Moses is an example of a name with an Egyptian theophoric element removed, and he suggests other names could have been similarly adjusted. Because the biblical record may preserve later Yahwistic forms, those names alone do not prove pre-exodus Yahweh worship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=9976" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:46:16</a> | Did Egypt or Israel have anything like chess? | He says no exact equivalent existed; Egyptian senet is the closest comparison, but it was a different kind of game. | He says there was no real equivalent to chess in Egypt or Israel. The closest Egyptian comparison is senet, a race-type board game with multiple pieces, but its rules are not fully known and it is not chess. He adds that chess itself developed much later in the Islamic period and was then adapted in Europe. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=10099" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:48:19</a> | How did the Israelites move the parts of the tabernacle? | He says the tabernacle was modular, built to be dismantled and carried in assigned sections by Levites. | He explains the tabernacle as a modular tent structure with beams, crossbeams, poles, and fabric that could be taken down, rolled up, carried, and reassembled. Different Levitical clans had different responsibilities: some set it up and took it down, others carried it, and Aaron's family handled priestly service. Its design was therefore meant for transport, not permanent construction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=10359" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:39</a> | Have you seen Galil's Facebook post about his readings of the tablets? | He says the readings look increasingly untenable to other scholars and describes the episode as a growing embarrassment. | He says the broader Galil situation is becoming embarrassing because scholars are increasingly saying they cannot see how his photographs support his readings. He mentions that Peter van der Veen no longer appears to agree with Galil on key details, undermining the claim of a unified epigraphic team. His assessment is that the claims are falling apart before peer-reviewed publication. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=10645" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:57:25</a> | What is your opinion of sand? | He says he likes sand, especially for excavation, and much prefers working in Egyptian sand to Israeli mud. | He says he likes sand because it is clean, easy to sweep, shovel, sift, and even use to rub dirt from the hands. He contrasts Egyptian sand with Israeli caked mud, which he describes as unpleasant to dig through, and says tar is worse still because it requires solvents. For an Egyptologist, he says liking sand is practically necessary. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=10770" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:59:30</a> | What is your opinion of near-death experiences? | He says near-death experiences are not actual death, often involve immediate terror before later reinterpretation, and remain medically and theologically unresolved. | He says near-death experiences are not full death because clinical death is not the same as neural death; the neural pathways have not degraded. He notes reports in which patients initially describe terror, torment, or fire shortly after revival, then later reinterpret the experience as beautiful. He therefore treats the phenomenon as real but unresolved, needing medical, psychological, and theological analysis rather than simple proof-texting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/llax2yc_y8w?t=11351" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:09:11</a> | Is it a sin for Christians to get tattoos? | He says not necessarily; the ancient ban was tied to ceremonial law and pagan dedication, so wisdom now depends on cultural context and conscience. | He explains the ancient prohibition in terms of tattoos as religious dedication marks to other gods, including examples from Egyptian priestesses. For Christians, he says the ceremonial law is covered by Christ, so tattoos are not inherently sinful. Whether getting one is wise depends on cultural meaning: a military tattoo may be harmless, a tattoo in a context where it signals organized crime may be unwise, and modern memorial tattoos raise questions of conscience and context. |
